speaker: interviewer
text: --in London. And this is Tape 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the two important events which left clear memories in my mind was, first of all, in March, 1938, when Hitler marched into Austria. History I don't wish to repeat. It's well recorded. We were sitting-- it happened to be on a Friday. We were sitting at the Friday-night dinner, being religious Jews. And having been so Orthodox Jews, we did not use cars-- we had access to cars-- or, for that reason, even electricity. We used to have a little clock which-- today, you use a proper time clock. But those days, you used one of these alarm clocks. And we had a little wire arrangement which then automatically switched it off at a certain hour. And then we had another one, which was attached to another light switch, which then turned it on at a given time. Now-- so whilst certain technical advances were not there, one could work one's way around. Anyway, on a Friday night, I heard Hitler's speech already, all what was happening. On that Friday, it was great excitement, the terrible events happening in Austria. My father's younger brother was in Austria, in Baden bei Wien, and some other relatives. And many, many people in our town had close relatives in-- across the border in Burgenland, which was practically a kilometer from the town center was the border. And I remember, suddenly a doorbell goes. And I, being the younger one-- I was eight years old-- I ran quickly to the lounge-room window, because we were sitting in the dining room, which was more to the garden, to see who the person is in front of the house. And I see a taxi, not stopping, but the engine still going. And a lady-- who was a spinster, I would practically describe her, certainly later on she never got married-- her name was Boros Rozsi-- came and desperately stood in front of the door and-- and kept ringing the bell.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father went down-- we lived in a house which had a mezzanine floor and had a drive in and it was like today you see in central European cities-- and asked her to come in. So she said that terrible events are happening. There are Jews on the border, which is literally a kilometer away. Have we got any blankets and food and whatnot? And which we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to me, it was a terrible shock. It was like something which I-- I couldn't imagine, having had an Orthodox religious upbringing, that another Orthodox person can turn up on the Shabbat in a car. However, these things eventually one understood what was going on. And that event is very clear in my mind, the shock of something terrible happening. Blankets, food is needed, because people are on the border. The rest of the week was a very traumatic, eventful week, hearing news of people being taken in Vienna to scrub the streets and so on. Don't forget, one of our premises was a huge garage premises, which was let out to a non-Jewish garage operator. And we were very friendly with them. And they kept going into Austria and Vienna on business regular, practically weekly. And they themselves told us what was going on with the Jews there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it make you feel, what-- being so close to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can probably put it down to the fact that I was very young and probably psychologically it didn't affect me all that much. I just thought it was a very exciting trouble. Something wasn't right. Something was-- But it didn't happen to us. You know, it was just a story, like a bad dream or a film you see, and then you leave the film, you shut-- and you go on home and you try to forget about it. But obviously, you couldn't forget about it, because it kept on recurring. And the next really impressive memory I have was not that Shabbat, the week after. The shul, the Orthodox shul, which I still have a picture of it, even though it became completely-- it was sort of neglected, they used it as a horses' stables during-- after the deportation and it's gone into neglect-- would show the size of the building. But there were a few hundred families who sort of lived in town, of which maybe the maximum attendance would have been about 200 family heads, maybe their parents or children and so on. But on that Shabbat, which was a week and a day after Hitler marched into Austria, the shul was full to capacity, which I never seen before or after. There wasn't a millimeter space. People stood practically outside. These were all Austrian Jewish refugees who managed to come immediately across the border, had relations in our town, and all tried to take refuge, which in fact they managed to do, because maybe some of them had some birthright of Hungarian birthright or something. Anyway, obviously-- And the one person who I can never get out of my mind-- face-- there was a thin, medium-built man. And I was told he was a rabbi of Kobersdorf. That also one of the [NON-ENGLISH]. The man was blue-and-black, beaten in his face, on his arm, his hands, swollen. I never forget. That is, I think, the first time that I came across real atrocity which I personally witnessed has happened to the person. I mean, I didn't see when they did it, but I saw it the aftereffects of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's interesting how-- I mean, I'm being of 66 years of age. To the best of my knowledge, I don't suffer of amnesia, so I do remember many things. But that is a particular picture which is clear in my own mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it didn't happen to me. It didn't happen to even a relation of mine. But this commotion, where people were milling around, I think the praying on that Shabbat was equal to zero, or maybe some did better than others. But it was like-- like a-- a refugee camp, it appeared to me. Anyway, matters seemed to have settled down, to the effect that many of them stayed in Sopron, in Hungary. The children quickly learned the language. And very relatively poor condition, depending who there relations were. And everybody tried to help them, whatever method. Because, I must say, the majority of the-- of the original Jewish population of the town were well-to-do. There were some poorer people. But you didn't see the poverty of Poland or Galicia or Russia in our town. There was no child who didn't have clothing or shoes. Maybe they didn't have from one week to the next more than what they could live on and they had to live on some sort of assistance of others. I remember, when it came to Purim time, to-- the custom was to [NON-ENGLISH], that we had a plate, a dinner plate. And my mother was an excellent-- she was a Cordon Bleu baker and very good cook. We had a very large household, foodwise and otherwise. And you took a few pieces of cake. You went-- as a child, you took it to the door. The person took it off and put on for you their cakes and you brought it home.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, to the poorer people, I remember there were big, like the American shopping bags, you know? These big, brown paper bags, five kilo of beans and flour and eggs and butter. And each family got a massive amount of that. You know, that was a gentlemanly way to give a present when it was appropriate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were obviously assisted by the community. And I think this point I didn't bring out when I referred to the autonomous Jewish community, its power and its infrastructure of having been built up in a relatively short time. I'm talking of basically 60 to 80 years, when the time of emancipation 'til the time of deportation. And many of these shuls were built within 20, 30 years after the emancipation, at the-- at the turn of the century or thereabouts, before the First World War.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was due to the fact that the autonomous Orthodox or Neolog communities had the right of every family and members of the family to pay communal taxes. These taxes were collectible by the community leadership in the same manner as income tax. The amount which they had to pay was delegated to a finance committee by the-- the main committee of that community, or synagogue if you like to call it. We didn't have [NON-ENGLISH] and so on. It was just the two communities, finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, whilst it was collectible legally through the courts, I think I didn't even hear if there ever came to it. However, as in every community, there are people who are not prepared to pay up. And usually the more they have, the less they pay. And there was this problem. Whilst the mainstream paid up, because one didn't want to-- people presumably didn't want to be put to shame that they didn't pay-- and they had the right of appeal, by the way, for the amount they were assessed. I mean, you know, they could bring their income tax returns. They could bring an inspection of the stocks of goods and approximate turnover. So it was really like an audit of the business if anybody wished to dispute the amount they were asked for. So it wasn't a free-for-all. I mean, it was relatively in an orderly, justifiable true fashion conducted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the interesting part was, obviously, there is always the odd person who doesn't pay. So what do you do with them? Now, the-- my father was all the years in the Orthodox community that I remember the head of the chevra kadisha, which is the burial society.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I have a very interesting photo which I cannot put my hands on, that it was customary for the chevra kadisha to hold an annual [NON-ENGLISH], a celebration, on the seventh of Adar, which is Moshe Rabbenu's [NON-ENGLISH]. And there used to be a nice, catered party given. And the custom in our town was that the members of the committee of the chevra kadisha-- obviously they were there, they led the whole proceedings-- turned up in a white kittel. You know, this is the white gown which you use on Yom Kippur or some-- we did it on Rosh Hashanah and on seder night, the person who gives the seder. And I thought this was a highly interesting custom, because these customs were not everywhere kept. These were individual customs. Where they originate from, I don't know. The only thing-- after the war, I-- since I lived in England, I've been to Gibraltar. And there's a surviving community of about 500 years old. And I found, interestingly, some customs which were very similar to the ones which we had. And I assume the reason for it must be that they-- the descendants of people who were chased out of Spain or Portugal and came back to Gibraltar, [INAUDIBLE] maybe others after the Inquisition, moved to the east, obviously they did to Italy, all the way to Constantinople, which is today Turkey. And also to our part-- certainly Holland, Germany, and then filtering more to the east. So I presume it has something to do with it. Now, so therefore, what happened was, the person died. Could be the head of the family or even a wife or a child. They had no chance to get buried anywhere else but only in the cemetery of the community, because nobody else would have touched them, not even the other Jewish community, for no money in the world. This was an implied agreement. You don't take refugees over, because for financial reasons, they want to run away from their responsibility.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was down to the chevra kadisha president and the treasurer to negotiate, during the time of sadness, when somebody just lost somebody overnight, that they had to pay up all the money they owed. And otherwise, there was no burial. Now, this may not be very kind. But, on the other hand, if you take into account that the community paid its rabbi, its hazzan, its Talmud Torah, its mikveh, its kashrut, all from the money which was paid and contributed by the individual members according to their financial status, it was only right that everybody should take their part.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was very jolly. He was an [INAUDIBLE] man. Used to say, my customers never complain, [LAUGHS] meaning that  the dead don't complain. But it was an unpleasant task, but being a communally inclined family, one has got to accept sometime unpleasant tasks, which was totally unpaid for. It was purely on an honorary basis. And probably it brought only more animosity than friends. But, on the other hand, the communal atmosphere was such that people understood that things were done genuinely for communal methods and not for personal gain. A, we didn't need it. And only people who were in relatively good financial circumstances could accept that position, because otherwise they would have been put under pressure that, look, if you do that to me, I'm not going to buy from you, and all that. Now my father, having been in the real estate business, when anybody wanted to have a house built for themselves or wanted to rent a property, it wasn't through-- and, presumably, the majority of the customers were non-Jewish, anyway. He wasn't in-- in need of the Jewish custom next morning whether they buy the fruits and bread and whatnots from us. Now, that brings me to the Jewish communal life. There were two Jewish bakeries. And we lived in the nice part of the town, which was starting on the bottom of the Alps. And the old part of the city and the synagogue, where it was even further than that, was not near to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it came to the question of kosher bread. And, being Orthodox, this was important. I remember that it was organized-- I mean, my grandfather, the farmer, so we had a tremendous larder and stocks of things-- that the bread was being prepared at home. We had a domestic. We had a manservant who did the more harder work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But whenever it was ready, the dough, then the girl or the man took it over to the bakery was around-- a non-Jewish bakery around the corner. And, according to Jewish law, all you had to do is to put a piece of wood in, and then all the baking was kosher. This is basically the-- the groundwork why Jewish bread can be baked in a non-Jewish bakery. I mean, no tins were used. It was an open-fire bakery, so from kashrut point of view, that wasn't interesting. Something else, which-- basically, just for the way of life it was. You know, cholent, people like it and do it. We didn't have, by the way, any gefilte fish. It didn't exist, because we had plenty of carp and good various fishes. So this, I presume, must have originated from areas where they had no fish or had no money to buy fish and they chopped up whatever bits of herring and whatnot so they created some sort of a mixture. And really it was only after the war when I left from there that I first seen gefilte fish, strange as it may sound. But the process of schooling verse a Shabbat, just that I can explain it to you. Friday night was the normal proceeding. You go to shul. You come home. You have your meal. Perfectly OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the food was kept warm in particular containers, because obviously we had a gas fire and a wood-- one of these which have become recently very modern-- these iron-- what is their name? An [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: A wood-burning stove.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it wasn't used, obviously, on Shabbat. So we had certain containers where the soup was kept, anything which was warm. Now Shabbat morning, you-- sorry. Friday night, Friday before Shabbat, you took a big pot. It was precooked a bit. And you put a newspaper on top of the lid and put a string and sealed it with a lead seal, and was taken to the kosher bakery, who kept it in the oven overnight. The next morning-- and there was no [NON-ENGLISH] in our town. And I often told that to rabbis, that today they would not allow it. And we had a perfectly-- Rabbi Posen, who was the son of Dayan Posen of Frankfurt, was an extremely-- he was a [NON-ENGLISH], even, but a very meticulous person-- allowed it. And there was no question. I never heard anything said against it, that on Shabbat, at lunchtime, you went along. There were two Jewish bakeries. Of course, one would have probably not been enough. And these pots were taken out by the bakery and were placed in a-- in a row there. And you had to recognize which pot was yours. And that's why they were sealed, so there should not be a mix-up, because the-- the seal, even though it was hot, it wasn't an ongoing fire. It was just kept at a certain temperature. They did not melt. Not even the paper burnt. It became sort of scorched, but it was still intact, and the string, as well. And then the domestic of the house went and fetched it. But somebody Jewish had to go with her, to make sure that nothing is tampered with the food 'til it gets home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must take you a few hours back. How was it on Shabbat morning? First of all, in the school-- I went to a non-Jewish school. There was Shabbat morning, we went to school like any other day. 8 o'clock in the morning, whatever week it was-- on Yom Kippur or Rosh Hashanah, we got exemptions. But on ordinary Shabbat, there was no exemption whatsoever.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened was that, at 8 o'clock in the morning, our domestic took my case-- or before 8-- and walked with me to school and placed my school bag down in school. We were exempted, even in the most anti-Semitic times, from writing. And they normally organize it that there were no written exams even, so as not to disturb the class, presumably. And so, therefore, boys above bar mitzvah age certainly could not attend to-- to Shabbat services in the winter, because it was too early. In the summer, it was all right, because we could start start [INAUDIBLE], say, at 6:30 in the morning so we were finished in time and had a quick breakfast. Now-- but the normal community, those who didn't go to school, the-- the shul started at 7:30 in the morning. By 9:15, 9:30, it was finished. We had breakfast. And after breakfast, you learned or you went for a walk or whatever. Usual lunchtime. And then, in the afternoon, the families used to get together in our garden or we went to them. You know, it was-- we lived very closely within the family and cousins and so on, as I have not enumerated, but I had two cousins of-- that was the middle brother, Julius, who was the one who became-- who survived. That was the only other Weiler who survived in our town. He was a professor. His sister, who did not survive. Then the lawyers had one son who was two years older me. He did also not survive. He was in the same camp as me. And my brother and myself. And this formed-- we were more or less the same age group, two years apart. And we used to play ping-pong and chess and football and, you know, the normal things which boys do, even these days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in addition-- so, therefore, how do you manage, foodwise on Shabbat? At least in our house and in most better apartments or flats, they had these tiled ovens I'm sure you have seen, which-- you know, they were going from floor to ceiling practically, or just below ceiling, and were wide.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the front you put in wood or coal, mainly wood. And the more modern ones could burn through for 24 hours. Now, some didn't, so those had on the top built in-- if you go to Eastern Europe or even to Vienna or to Austria, you will see. They call them kachelofen. They were very beautiful, sort of colorful, with motives on the stoves. But some of them just had enough fuel in it to last overnight. But by the morning, the fire went out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it had an opening like a chromium door, quite a deep door. And it was customary and acceptable in Orthodox circles that you took milk in a pot. You put it in before the domestic or the non-Jewish person lit the fire for the purposes of heating the room. And it was perfectly acceptable, and that way we had hot water and hot milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that is just giving you, by the way, how lifestyle went. In the afternoon, we used to go out in the-- in the nearby forest, which this was a holiday resort as well, besides having had five universities. It was a very pretty town, and it can be still inspected today, except there are no Jews left there. I mean, my only connection with it is my two grandparents of my father's parents, who died before I was born. Their graves are there. And strangely enough, they remained intact. And they have beautiful, large, black granite stones, which I somewhat had, with my cousin together, put in more solid [INAUDIBLE], but they were always there. They died in the late '20s. A few years ago, I read that there were some vandalism in that cemetery, even so, they had a permanent caretaker there all the years, from '45 during the Communist times, even today. I knew the guy, because I went there a few times after 30 years when I went back to Hungary. And there was some vandalism. You wouldn't believe it, what the story was. The story was that these were Germans who came in from Austria to vandalize a cemetery in Hungary. I mean, such a story put on its head. Those German or Austrian hooligans find enough Jewish cemeteries where they can do damage locally. They don't have to come into another country to do it. But this is the method and the-- today's mentality, even, it had nothing to do with us. We are not at fault. It's always the other one. [LAUGHS] It is true to say that the main [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] source of serious anti-Semitism, pogroms, and so on originated from the north, starting from Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia, Poland, Ukraine underlined three times, Slovakia, Hungary, Croatia, Austria particularly, and southern Germany, Bayern, and especially if you go down to Sudtirol, which is called, which has always been a hotbed of Nazism. They've got one thing in common. They are all Catholic. I'm not saying that the Lutherans were particularly favorable to Jews. But this rampant incitement may not have taken as much place. I remember I had friends who were Protestants. And maybe it was individual. I don't know. But they-- and also maybe because they were also a minority in the country, they were not so-- taking such pleasures to be anti-Semitic. And I must say that even in the school as such, I had a perfectly amiable relationship with my school friends. And I remember only one case when there was one boy, who, in fact, didn't even come from that town, he came outside from some rural district, once made a remark. And even though I knew that they were in the majority, there were some other friends of mine who took my side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you see, it's very difficult to explain that you live in a seriously anti-Semitic time, but there are certain signs and events which make you feel to become perfectly complacent that all is well. It will all pass by. Not all are bad-- which is true. But, of course, the management from the top knew what they were doing, and it was a systematic way to drive in the direction which led to the Holocaust, basically.
